fr:mainmatter.html:p: A precategory C is specified by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each x\in C, an identity arrow 1_{x}\colon x\to x;
for each f\colon x\to y and g\colon y\to z, a composite arrow g\circ f\colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
\boldsymbol {\lambda }_{f}\colon 1_{y}\circ f = f\quad \boldsymbol {\rho }_{f}\colon f\circ 1_{x} = f \quad \boldsymbol {\alpha }_{f,g,h}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen h\circ g\right \rparen \mathclose {} \circ f = h\circ \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A functor F\colon C\to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

html:ol.html:li:
such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions:
F^{1_{x}}\colon F1_{x} = 1_{Fx}\quad F^{g\circ f}\colon F \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {} = Fg\circ Ff

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A functor F\colon C \to D is called (full, faithful) when the action on arrows is valued in (surjections, embeddings) respectively. It is common to refer to a full and faithful functor as fully faithful.
Fullness and faithfulness are both properties of a functor.
A functor F\colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f\colon C\cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
A section-retraction pair in a precategory C consists of an arrow s\colon x \to y called the section and an arrow r\colon y \to x called the retraction such that r\circ s = 1_{x}.
An arrow f\colon x \to y in a precategory C is called an isomorphism when either of there exists an inverse to f, i.e. an arrow g\colon y \to x that is simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
We shall write f\colon x \cong y to denote an isomorphism from x to y.
A category is defined to be a univalent precategory.
The material on univalent category theory is drawn primarily from the HoTT Book and Ahrens, Kapulkin, and Shulman.

fr:title.text: Univalent categories and the Rezk completion

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Univalent categories and the Rezk completion
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @article{ahrens-kapulkin-shulman-2015, title={Univalent categories and the Rezk completion}, volume={25}, doi={10.1017/S0960129514000486}, number={5}, journal={Mathematical Structures in Computer Science}, publisher={Cambridge University Press}, author={Ahrens, Benedikt and Kapulkin, Krzysztof and Shulman, Michael}, year={2015}, pages={1010–1039} }

fr:title.text: Homotopy Type Theory: univalent foundations of mathematics

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Homotopy Type Theory: univalent foundations of mathematics
fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{hottbook, author = {{Univalent Foundations Program}, The}, address = {Institute for Advanced Study}, publisher = {\url{https://homotopytypetheory.org/book}}, year = {2013}, title = {Homotopy Type Theory: Univalent Foundations of Mathematics}, }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Homotopy type theory is a new branch of mathematics that combines aspects of several different fields in a surprising way. It is based on a recently discovered connection between homotopy theory and type theory. It touches on topics as seemingly distant as the homotopy groups of spheres, the algorithms for type checking, and the definition of weak \infty -groupoids. Homotopy type theory offers a new “univalent” foundation of mathematics, in which a central role is played by Voevodsky’s univalence axiom and higher inductive types. The present book is intended as a first systematic exposition of the basics of univalent foundations, and a collection of examples of this new style of reasoning — but without requiring the reader to know or learn any formal logic, or to use any computer proof assistant. We believe that univalent foundations will eventually become a viable alternative to set theory as the “implicit foundation” for the unformalized mathematics done by most mathematicians.
The fiber of a function f\colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a\colon A equipped with identifications p\colon fa=b.
When s\colon A\to B is a section of r\colon B\to A, we say that A is a retract of B.
A biinverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with a biinverse as a biinvertible function.

A quasi-inverse to a function f \colon A \to B is a function g \colon B \to A equipped with homotopies \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{B} and \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{A}, rendering g simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
Although no coherence laws are assumed for \eta and \epsilon , we shall refer to them respectively as the unit and counit of the quasi-inverse.

An inverse to a function f\colon A \to B is defined to be a quasi-inverse equipped with an additional higher coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f in f\circ g \circ f \sim f relating the (a priori incoherent) unit and the counit of the quasi-inverse. We will refer to a function f\colon A\to B equipped with an inverse as an invertible function.
A function f\colon A\to B is called an embedding when for any x,y:A the action on identifications \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x=y\to fx=fy is invertible.
An equivalence from A to B is defined to be a function A \to B equipped with a biinverse.

Let H \colon f \sim g be a homotopy between two functions f,g\colon A \to B. For any identification p\colon x =y in A, we have an identification H x \cdot \mathsf {act}_{g}\,p = \mathsf {act}_{f}\,p\cdot Hy.
By identification induction on p, it suffices to show that Hx\cdot \mathsf {refl} = \mathsf {refl}\cdot Hx.

fr:title.text: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Naturality of homotopies onto the identity

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let H \colon f \sim 1_{A} be a homotopy from f\colon A \to A onto the identity function. Then we may construct a higher homotopy from H\circ f to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ H.
Any quasi-invertible map f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
The quasi-inverse to f is simultaneously a section and a retraction.

Let f \colon A \to B have a quasi-inverse g\colon B\to A with incoherent counit \epsilon \colon f\circ g\sim 1_{B} and unit \eta \colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A}. We can correct the counit to obtain a coherent inverse to f\colon A\to B.
The corrected counit \epsilon ' \colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} is defined like so:
The coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta \sim \epsilon '\circ f can be constructed using our two naturality lemmas Lemma and Lemma .

Let f\colon A\to B be biinvertible with s,r\colon B\to A and h_s\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} and h_r\colon r\circ f = 1_{A}. Then we may define a homotopy h' \colon s\circ f \sim 1_{A} exhibiting s\colon B\to A as a retraction of f\colon A\to B. Therefore, we may define a quasi-inverse to f tracked by s\colon B\to A.
We have s\circ f \sim r\circ f \circ s\circ f by h_r and r\circ f \circ s\circ f\sim r \circ f by h_s and r\circ f \sim 1_{A} by h_r.
Let f\colon A \to B and g\colon B\to C be maps equipped with sections s_f\colon B\to A and s_g\colon C\to B. The composite s_f\circ s_g\colon C\to A is a section of g\circ f\colon A \to C.
The witness of sectionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two sections.
Let f\colon A \to B and g\colon B\to C be maps equipped with retractions r_f\colon B\to A and r_g\colon C\to B. The composite r_f\circ r_g\colon C\to A is a retraction of g\circ f\colon A \to C.
The witness of retractionhood is defined using the witnesses for the two retractions.

Let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to C be biinvertible maps. Then the composite g\circ f\colon A \to C is biinvertible, with sections and retractions given by composites of the component sections and retractions.

Let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to C be quasi-invertible maps. Then the composite g\circ f\colon A \to C is quasi-invertible, with sectionhood and retractionhood witnesses given by those of the composites of the component sections and retractions.
Let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to C be invertible maps. Then the composite g\circ f\colon A\to C is invertible.
By Lemma , it suffices to find a quasi-inverse to the composite g\circ f; we take this to be the composite (Construction ) of the underlying quasi-inverses of f and g.
Let s\colon A\to B exhibit A as a retract of B; then \mathsf {act}_{s}\colon x = y\to sx=sy exhibits x=y as a retract of sx=sy for each x,y:A.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x\colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A type A is called a set when for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is a proposition.
The notions of contractible and propositional types generate an infinite hierarchy of truncation levels expressing the point after which higher identifications become trivial.
A truncation level is an integer n \geq -2.
Given a truncation level n, we define what it means for a type to be n-truncated by induction:

html:ul.html:li: a type is -2-truncated when it is contractible;
a type is -1-truncated when it is a proposition;
for n \geq 0, a type A is n-truncated when for every x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-1-truncated.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: So, in particular, a 0-truncated type is precisely a set. We shall often refer to n-truncated types as n-types.

A function f\colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as propositional when their fibers are propositions.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y\colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.
If a type A is a proposition, then it is a set.
If A is a proposition, then for all x,y:A the identification type x=y is contractible.
The centre of contraction of x=y is given by our assumption that A is a proposition; the contracting identifications are obtained from the same assumption via Lemma .
If A is n+1-truncated, then for each x,y:A the identification type x=y is n-truncated.
By induction on n; the base case for n=-2 is given by Corollary , and the other cases are trivial.
If a type A is n-truncated, then it is also n+1-truncated.
By induction on the truncation level n, with two base cases given by Lemma and Lemma .
For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is contractible.
Immediate by identification induction.

fr:title.text: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The identity map is n-truncated for all n

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For any type A, the identity map 1_{A}\colon A\to A is n-truncated for all n.

We proceed by induction on the truncation level n: when n=-2, we recall that the identity map is contractible, and in the inductive step we apply the upward closure of n-truncation to each fiber of 1_{A}.
If B is a retract of A and A is a proposition, then B is a proposition.
This can be seen using the cancellation laws of retractions.

fr:title.text: contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: contractible types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.html:p: If B is a retract of A and A is contractible, then B is a contractible.
Let s\colon B \to A be a section of r\colon A\to B; if a_0 is the centre of contraction of A, then the centre of contraction of B is given by ra_0. For the rest, we use Lemma and Lemma .

fr:title.text: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: n-truncated types are closed under retracts

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Any retract of an n-truncated type is n-truncated.

By induction on n; the base cases for n=-2 and n=-1 are given by Corollary and Lemma respectively. In the inductive step, we assume that s\colon B\to A exhibits B as a retract of A and that for each x,y:A the identification type x=_Ay is n+1-truncated to show that for all u,v:B the identification type u=_Bv is likewise n+1-truncated. The result follows from our assumption and the fact that u=_Bv is (by Lemma ) a retract of su=_Asv.
When we have an element of \mathsf {toProp}\,A, we will say that A is inhabited.
A type A is called inhabited when we have an element of \mathsf {toProp}\,A. When we say there exists, we refer to inhabitation.
A function f\colon A\to B is called surjective when for any b\colon B, the fiber of f over b is inhabited.
A function f\colon A\to B is called bijective when it is a surjective propositional map.

Let \chi _x \colon a = x be the witness of contraction for any x:A. Given b : Ba and x:A, we define \mathsf {ind}_{A,a}\,b\,x : Bx by transporting b along the identification \chi _a^{-1} \cdot \chi _x \colon a = x:

fr:title.text: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path objects and the univalence axiom

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a reflexive graph, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed reflexive graph over \mathcal {A}. The total reflexive graph of \mathcal {B} over \mathcal {A}, written \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B}, is defined as follows:

fr:title.text: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The discrete reflexive graph on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two reflexive graphs. We define the binary product of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} to be the following reflexive graph:

fr:title.text: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Product of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coproduct of a family of reflexive graphs

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We will write \mathsf {PathObj} for the (large) type of path objects. In our notation, we will at times silently coerce a given path object to its underlying type of vertices.
Univalence follows from univalence of \mathcal {A} and the fact that B is valued in propositions.

fr:title.text: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic path object on a type

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The discrete reflexive graph on a type A is a path object, which we shall refer to as the intrinsic path object on A.
Univalence follows immediately by identification induction.
The reflexive graph structure on types given by equivalences is univalent. We shall write \mathsf {Type} to indicate the resulting (large) path object of types and equivalences.

fr:title.text: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The univalence principle for dependent functions

fr:mainmatter.html:p: This follows immediately from univalence for functions.
The dependent product of a family of propositions is a proposition.
The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .
Let \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B} be two path objects; then the binary product \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} is a path object.
The univalence property of \mathcal {A}\times \mathcal {B} follows immediately from that of \mathcal {A} and \mathcal {B}.

html:ol.html:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.
We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:title.text: display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of left-cloven families of path objects

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: display of right-cloven families of path objects

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

fr:title.text: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:title.text: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:title.text: path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: path object structure on reflexive graphs

html:ol.html:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

fr:title.text: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Further closure properties of truncation levels

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is contractible.
The first and second steps follow from general computation lemmas for transport, and the last step follows from the fact that A is a set, which we may deduce from Lemma and Lemma .

fr:title.text: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a proposition is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: It is a proposition that a given type A is a proposition.
It is a proposition that a given type A is n-truncated.
We proceed by induction on the truncation level n.

html:ol.html:li: When n=-2, we recall from Lemma that being contractible is a proposition.
When n=-1, we recall from Lemma that being a proposition is a proposition.

fr:title.text: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an n-truncated map is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon A \to B is n-truncated is a proposition.

fr:title.text: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being a surjection is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given map f\colon E\to B is surjective is a proposition.
By Corollary .
Any invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a section.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a retraction r\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r\colon r\circ f\sim 1_{A} exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f\colon A\to B be contractible; we define a section s\colon B\to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s\colon f\circ s\sim 1_{B} exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
Any contractible map f\colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.
Any quasi-invertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.
Analogous to the corresponding result for pushforward along biinvertible maps.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A \to B is propositional.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is surjective.
This is immediate, using the unit map of propositional truncation and the section of f.
Any biinvertible map f\colon A\to B is a bijection.
By Lemma and Corollary .
Any embedding f\colon A\to B is propositional.
Let \alpha \colon fa_0 = fa_1 be the concatenation p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Therefore we may define:

To define \psi \colon p_0=\mathsf {act}_{f}\phi \cdot p_1 we use the counit of our inverse as we have \epsilon \alpha \colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\phi = p_0\cdot p_1^{-1}. Thus concatenating on either side with p_1 we have our goal, modulo the laws of identification concatenation and symmetry respectively.
Any propositional map f\colon A\to B is an embedding.

Fixing x,y:A we must show that \mathsf {act}_{f}\colon x = y \to fx=fy is invertible. By Lemma and Lemma , it suffices to exhibit a biinverse to \mathsf {act}_{f} (it is convenient that the underlying maps of the section and retraction need not be identical).
Any biinvertible map is an embedding.
By Lemma and Lemma .
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is propositional by Lemma , the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any bijection f\colon A\to B is biinvertible.
By Lemma , bijections are contractible; by Corollary , bijections are contractible.

fr:title.text: Coherence of different notions of equivalence

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Coherence of different notions of equivalence

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let f\colon A\to B be a section of some r\colon B\to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f\colon A\to B is a retract of the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B}, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. By Lemma , it suffices to show that the fiber of f\circ - at 1_{B} is a proposition, which follows via Lemma and Lemma .
Let f\colon A\to B be a retraction of some s\colon B\to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of -\circ f at 1_{A}.
The type of biinverses of a given function f\colon A\to B is a proposition.
Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section

fr:title.text: coherent remainder as a path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: coherent remainder as a path object

html:li.html:p: We exhibit the latter as a right-cloven family of path objects over the former with the cleaving that for \eta _0,\eta _1\colon g\circ f\sim 1_{A} and \eta _{01}\colon \eta _0 \sim \eta _1 defines \eta _{01}^*\colon \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _1\sim \epsilon \circ f \to \mathsf {act}_{f}\circ \eta _0\sim \epsilon \circ f as follows:

fr:mainmatter.html:p: With this cleaving in mind, finally define \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma to be the following contravariant sum of path objects:

fr:title.text: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The coherent remainder of a section is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: By Lemma , we may argue that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of a proposition. We let P be the following type defined using path object structure of fibers given in Example :
It is not difficult to show using Definition that \mathsf {coherentRemainder}_{f}\sigma is a retract of P; we will not need the further fact that it is equivalent to P.
For any function f\colon A\to B, the type of inverses to f is a proposition.

fr:title.text: Being an embedding is a proposition

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Being an embedding is a proposition

fr:mainmatter.html:p: That a given function f\colon A \to B is an embedding is a proposition.
By Corollary and Corollary .
That a given map f\colon A \to B is bijective is a proposition.
By Lemma , Lemma via Lemma .
A precategory C is specified by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each x\in C, an identity arrow 1_{x}\colon x\to x;
for each f\colon x\to y and g\colon y\to z, a composite arrow g\circ f\colon x \to z;

such that composition is associative and unital with respect to identity arrows:
\boldsymbol {\lambda }_{f}\colon 1_{y}\circ f = f\quad \boldsymbol {\rho }_{f}\colon f\circ 1_{x} = f \quad \boldsymbol {\alpha }_{f,g,h}\colon \mathopen {}\left \lparen h\circ g\right \rparen \mathclose {} \circ f = h\circ \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {}

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A functor F\colon C\to D from a precategory C to a precategory D is defined by the following data:

html:ol.html:li:
such that the action on morphisms commutes with identities and compositions:
F^{1_{x}}\colon F1_{x} = 1_{Fx}\quad F^{g\circ f}\colon F \mathopen {}\left \lparen g\circ f\right \rparen \mathclose {} = Fg\circ Ff

fr:mainmatter.html:p: A functor F\colon C \to D is called (full, faithful) when the action on arrows is valued in (surjections, embeddings) respectively. It is common to refer to a full and faithful functor as fully faithful.
Fullness and faithfulness are both properties of a functor.
A functor F\colon C \to D is called an isomorphism, written f\colon C\cong D, when F is fully faithful and its action on objects is an equivalence of types.
Being an isomorphism is a proposition.

The type of isomorphisms onto C is inhabited, considering the identity isomorphism 1_{C}\colon C\cong C. Therefore, it suffices to show that the type of isomorphisms onto C depicted below is a proposition:
We unfold the definitions of a precategories and their isomorphisms, dropping propositional axioms:
A function that is both propositional and a surjection, i.e. a bijection, is an equivalence by Corollary . We can therefore repackage our telescope equivalently:
Next we rewrite using the univalence principles for functions and families of types (Axiom , Axiom ):
By the univalence principle for functions, the data above is contractible.
A section-retraction pair in a precategory C consists of an arrow s\colon x \to y called the section and an arrow r\colon y \to x called the retraction such that r\circ s = 1_{x}.
An arrow f\colon x \to y in a precategory C is called an isomorphism when either of there exists an inverse to f, i.e. an arrow g\colon y \to x that is simultaneously a section and a retraction of f.
We shall write f\colon x \cong y to denote an isomorphism from x to y.
A category is defined to be a univalent precategory.
The material on univalent category theory is drawn primarily from the HoTT Book and Ahrens, Kapulkin, and Shulman.
A displayed precategory C over a precategory B, written B\vdash C, is specified by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each b.c\in C a displayed identity arrow 1_{b}.1_{c}\colon b.c\to b.c;
such that composition is associative and unital with respect to displayed identity arrows.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Given two displayed objects b.c,b.c' in a displayed precategory B\vdash C, we shall refer to displayed arrows of the form 1_{b}.f\colon b.c\to b.c' as vertical.

Let B\vdash C be a displayed precategory. We define the fiber of C over b\in B to be the precategory b.C whose objects are given by displayed objects b.c\in C and whose arrows b.c_0\to b.c_1 are given by vertical displayed arrows 1_{b}.f\colon b.c_0\to b.c_1, with identities and compositions inherited from C.
A displayed precategory B\vdash C is called univalent when each of its fiber precategories b.C is univalent.
A displayed category is defined to be a univalent displayed precategory.
Given a displayed precategory B\vdash C, we may define a functor \pi _{B\vdash C}\colon B.C\to B called the display functor, sending b.c \in B.C to b and i.f\colon b.c\to b'.c' to i\colon b\to b'.

fr:title.text: Conditions for the total precategory to be univalent

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Conditions for the total precategory to be univalent

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let B\vdash C be a precategory displayed over a precategory B. If B is univalent and B\vdash C is univalent, then the total precategory B.C is univalent.

fr:title.text: univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: univalence of the total precategory vs. the fibers

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Give an example of a displayed category B\vdash C over a precategory B such that the total precategory B.C is univalent but B is nonetheless not univalent.
Let B\vdash C,D be two displayed precategories. A vertical displayed functor from C to D over B, written B.F \colon C\to D, is given by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each displayed object b.c\in C, a displayed object b.Fc \in D;
for each displayed arrow i.f\colon b_0.c_0 \to b_1.c_1 in C, a displayed arrow i.Ff\colon b_0.Fc_0\to b_1.Fc_1 in D;
such the assignment of displayed arrows preserves displayed identities and displayed composites.

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let B.F, B.G\colon C\to D be two vertical displayed functors between two displayed precategories B\vdash C,D. A vertical displayed natural transformation from B.F to B.G over B, written B.\alpha \colon B.F\to B.G, is given by the following data:

html:ol.html:li: for each displayed object b.c\in C, a vertical arrow b.\alpha _c\colon b.Fc\to b.Gc;

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The material on displayed categories is drawn from Ahrens and Lumsdaine, although we have imposed our own terminological and notational conventions.

The purpose of displayed categories is to define category-like objects that depend on other categories. We will later on learn how to impose conditions that make displayed categories behave like (potentially large) categories internal to other categories. In order to make the theory sufficiently flexible, however, it is necessary to first relativise the notion of displayed category to allow the base to be another (relative) displayed category rather than an ordinary category. The starting point of our approach to relativisation is the insight that a category is a special case of displayed category.
A displayed category over the terminal category gives precisely the data of a category.

In light of the observation above, the base of a displayed category in the sense that we have described so far is another displayed category — whose base is the terminal category. This suggests a simple way to expand our vocabulary to account for displayed categories whose base is another displayed category.

Let B\vdash C displayed precategory over a precategory B. A relative displayed precategory over C, written C\vdash E, is defined to be a displayed precategory B.C\vdash E over the total precategory B.C. A relative displayed category over C is then a displayed category over B.C.
When it is clear from context what we mean, we will speak of displayed categories over C, as in what follows, the relative version of displayed categories will be the primary one.

fr:title.text: The relative displayed precategory of slices

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The relative displayed precategory of slices

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let B\vdash C be a displayed precategory; we may define a relative displayed precategory C\vdash C _{/} as follows:

html:ol.html:li: A displayed object b.c.p\in C _{/} is given by a vertical arrow over b\in B with codomain b.c, i.e. an arrow p\colon \partial _0 p\to c in the fiber precategory b.C.
